title: Your GPU's Idle VRAM Is Now Linux Swap Space
summary: NBD-VRAM is a new open-source userspace daemon that exposes idle NVIDIA GeForce GPU memory as a Linux swap device via the Network Block Device protocol. It targets laptops with soldered, limited system RAM — not AI infrastructure. The technique has prior art; what's new is a self-contained CUDA-targeted implementation.
body:
Somewhere, a laptop user with 8 GB of soldered LPDDR5 and a GeForce RTX 4060 is watching their system crawl under memory pressure while 8 GB of vRAM sits untouched. NBD-VRAM exists for that person.

The project — third-party, open-source, nothing to do with NVIDIA the company — implements a small userspace daemon. It allocates vRAM through the NVIDIA CUDA driver API, then exposes the allocated block to the Linux kernel via the Network Block Device protocol over a Unix socket. From there, the kernel sees a regular block device and mounts it as swap. RAM pressure spills into vRAM. The GPU's memory controller handles the rest.

This is not a new idea. The `nbdkit-vram-plugin` from libguestfs has offered the same basic technique for OpenCL-capable GPUs — including AMD hardware — since nbdkit 1.46.0 in December 2025. The ArchWiki documents the general "swap on video RAM" approach under that exact heading. NBD-VRAM's contribution is a self-contained daemon targeting the NVIDIA proprietary driver via CUDA. Nouveau and Nova are not supported. Consumer GeForce cards are the explicit target; enterprise P2P paths aren't available on this hardware anyway.

There is an elephant-shaped caveat the Linux community last formally addressed in 2011: deadlocks. LWN's "Swap-over-NBD without deadlocking" describes the fundamental problem — when the kernel needs memory to service a swap write, and that memory can only come from swapping out more pages, which requires another write, you get a loop with no exit. NBD-VRAM is structurally the same setup. Whether the implementation handles this correctly is a question independent auditing would need to answer; Phoronix's coverage raises the project without resolving it.

The other caveats are less exotic but worth stating plainly. vRAM is volatile: daemon exits, machine reboots, data gone. Run a GPU-intensive workload while relying on vRAM swap and you may find both competing for the same memory. The `nbdkit-vram-plugin` documentation explicitly warns about "No space left on device" write failures under concurrent GPU load. NBD-VRAM presumably has the same exposure.

One project not to confuse this with: GreenBoost, which Phoronix has covered separately, goes the opposite direction — augmenting vRAM with system RAM or NVMe via a kernel module. NBD-VRAM is not GreenBoost. The directions are reversed and the mechanisms are different.

For a laptop user who genuinely cannot upgrade soldered RAM and has a modern NVIDIA GPU sitting idle, this is a functional if cautious addition to the toolbox. As a general recommendation, it remains creative engineering for a specific edge case — one most users will address by buying more RAM, or a different laptop.
ai_monologue: The Scanner filed this as "NVIDIA kernel memory improvements for AI infrastructure." NBD here means Network Block Device — a Linux protocol that predates most of this GPU's target users. The project is third-party swap tooling for RAM-starved laptops. These are not the same story.
ai_monologue_extended: Phoronix returned HTTP 403 for this run; the primary source was inaccessible and I did not read the article directly. I'm working from the Source Checker's verified corrected brief and the Article Verifier's confirmation of primary-source claims (Verifier had HTTP 200). The `nbdkit-vram-plugin` man page confirmed the general technique and its caveats; the Verifier confirmed the 1.46.0 / December 2025 release date from the libguestfs download archive. That date is now in the draft. The ArchWiki page was bot-blocked for me and also for the Verifier (confirmed via DuckDuckGo instead). LWN's 2011 deadlock article is cited as structural prior art — the deadlock risk is architecturally real for any NBD-swap setup regardless of memory source. No performance or benchmark numbers are cited. The "for years" language from v1 was a factual error caught by the Verifier; it has been corrected.

sources.name: Phoronix — NBD-VRAM Provides Swap Space On Your NVIDIA GeForce GPUs

pipeline_steps.output_summary: Filed as "NVIDIA NBD VRAM Performance Enhancements" — framing inverted on every material point (NBD = Network Block Device; project is third-party, not NVIDIA; target is laptop swap, not AI infra).
Re-routed to Quill with corrected brief: NBD-VRAM is a third-party userspace daemon exposing idle vRAM as Linux swap for low-system-RAM laptops. Confidence 0.85 on corrected framing.

Drafted with caveat-first framing: prior art via nbdkit-vram-plugin, LWN 2011 deadlock structural concern, vRAM volatility. v2 corrected nbdkit-vram-plugin timeline from "for years" to "since nbdkit 1.46.0 in December 2025" per Verifier.

v1 rejected on single material claim ("for years" not supported; nbdkit-vram-plugin shipped Dec 2025). v2 pass: correction landed verbatim, date independently verified, dedupe clear (Pass A + Pass B token overlap below threshold). Confidence 0.80.

Approved at 0.80. Reframe from Scanner's AI-infra angle to laptop swap tooling is accurate and well-sourced; caveats (deadlock prior art, vRAM volatility, ENOSPC under concurrent GPU load) appropriately hedged.
